elements.sent-2411-001: State-of-the-art algorithms are reported to be almost perfect at distinguishing the vibrations arising from healthy and damaged machine bearings, according to benchmark datasets at least.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, what about their application to new data? In this paper, we are able to confirm that neural networks for bearing fault detection can be crippled by incorrect hyperparameterisation, and also that the correct hyperparameter settings can actually change when transitioning to new data.
elements.sent-2411-003: The paper weaves together multiple methods to explain the behaviour of the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel convolutional neural network and how to set them.
elements.sent-2411-004: Since guidance already exists for generic hyperparameters like minibatch size, we focus on how to set architecture-specific hyperparameters such as the width of the convolutional kernels, a topic which might otherwise be obscure.
elements.sent-2411-005: We reflect different data properties by fusing information from seven different benchmark datasets, and our results show that the kernel size in the first layer in particular is sensitive to changes in the data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Looking deeper, we use manipulated copies of one dataset in an attempt to spot why the kernel size sometimes needs to change.
elements.sent-2411-007: The relevance of sampling rate is studied by using different levels of resampling, and spectral content is studied by increasingly filtering out high frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-008: At the end of our paper we conclude by stating clear guidance on how to set the hyperparameters of our neural network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-009: Machines are full of bearings, the components that make rotation possible.
elements.sent-2411-010: They are small metal balls usually arranged in a circular casing and by rolling, these spheres allow one piece of a machine to move around relative to another piece of the machine.
elements.sent-2411-011: Wear and tear unfortunately can accumulate over time into damage that causes the bearing element to ultimately break down, taking the entire machine offline with it.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, one way this can happen is if small scratches develop in the casing that holds the bearings in place.
elements.sent-2411-013: As the ball bearings roll over that scratch again and again (perhaps hundreds of times per minute for days, weeks and months), it can grow into a structural weakness.
elements.sent-2411-014: One day, the casing can crack due to this weakness, breaking the rotating element and putting the entire machine out of commission until repairs are made.
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, methods for detecting such faults are crucial.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper -- a significantly extended and adapted revision of~\cite{hudson2024stay building on our work presented in~\cite{hoogen2023hyperparams -- we tackle the question of how to configure neural networks so that they can effectively identify different types of damaged bearings.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Thus, in this paper we do not start from scratch but instead our work takes place after and continues from research we published earlier.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, this paper presents a series of new experiments that address questions raised and left unresolved by our earlier work in~\cite{hudson2024stay and \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Two new neural network architectures are studied in order to show how important hyperparameter configuration is to a wider range of networks.
elements.sent-2411-020: We present a novel experimentation methodology, extending the methodology presented in~\cite{hoogen2023hyperparams , for assessing the impact of hyperparameters in the context of wide-kernel approaches for bearing fault detection.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Doing that, we run new experiments on manipulated subsets of the data to see how dataset properties affect the key question of how to configure hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, we present more datasets, more neural network architectures and more extensive results compared to \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams .}
elements.sent-2411-023: From outside of the machine, the damage that develops in a bearing element cannot be seen.
elements.sent-2411-024: Bearings are encased within the machine and hidden from view.
elements.sent-2411-025: This creates obvious problems for monitoring the accumulation of damage within a bearing element and deciding when a replacement is needed.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, using vibrations which spread through the machine, we can try to look inside at what is happening: sensors placed on the outside of the machine can pick up vibrations that originate from deep inside.
elements.sent-2411-027: This is an approach that gives some hope of detecting when replacements are needed before a machine has an unexpected breakdown.
elements.sent-2411-028: Unfortunately, vibrations bring their own problems since reading these signals is not like looking at a photograph, and instead a lot of work is needed to interpret the vibrations that are being gathered by the sensors.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is in particular relevant to initiatives such as Industry 4.0~\cite{wahlster2012industry,kagermann2022ten , \eg for enabling predictive maintenance applications~\cite{zonta2020predictive , for which also explainable and interpretable approaches are usually beneficial~\cite{VAT:21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: One way to interpret vibration data and figure out how to distinguish healthy and damaged bearings is to learn from experience, by applying machine learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-031: Neural networks in particular are machine learning methods that can be very successfully trained by exposing them to a dataset of examples.
elements.sent-2411-032: As summarised in \cite{neupane2024data , various types of neural networks have been reported to achieve 97\%+ accuracy on fault detection tasks, in papers such as \cite{zhang2018deep, eren2017bearing, guo2016hierarchical, han2021combination .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, we also developed various deep learning algorithms to improve performance in fault detection, as described in \cite{hoogen2020improved, hoogen2021classifying .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In the pursuit of effective algorithms to recognise when damage has occurred to a bearing element, researchers have settled on a series of `benchmark' datasets, used to train and test different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-035: The obvious benefit of these openly-available datasets is that it is like having a fair race: everyone is starting at the same place and running the same route, so we can compare algorithms fairly based on their performance on these benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-036: In our work, we make use of seven datasets originating from different organisations: Case Western Reserve University (CWRU), Paderborn University, the so-called `Gearbox' dataset, the Society for Machine Failure Prevention Technology (MFPT), University of Conneticut (UoC), Southeast University (SEU) and the Xi'an Jiaotong University (XJTU).
elements.sent-2411-037: Looking at past results on benchmark datasets, it has become obvious that multiple successful deep learning approaches exist.
elements.sent-2411-038: A recent review of the literature on the topic \cite{neupane2024data indicates that at least 25 different deep learning algorithms have achieved over 95\% accuracy (often 99\% or 100\%) on one or more of the benchmark datasets mentioned above.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Such consistently high levels of performance imply that the difference between two state-of-the-art algorithms is now predestined to be small and there is little room left to improve beyond previous results.
elements.sent-2411-040: Therefore, comparing one state-of-the-art option to another is becoming less interesting and important than before.
elements.sent-2411-041: Previous research in bearing fault detection has focused almost exclusively on finding algorithmic innovations with the hope of achieving higher scores during testing; however, since there is little room to improve, we now propose that the salient question is not whether new algorithms can be found, but whether the existing algorithms will continue to perform well when applied to new, unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-042: This is the question of whether they will still achieve near-perfect results when someone applies them in a real-life setting, when the sensors and machines are different from what the benchmarks used.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Limits of What the Benchmarks Can Tell Us
elements.sent-2411-043: Benchmarks are very useful when comparing algorithms but they can only give an indication about how successful a method will be when applied to new data in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-044: The belief that an algorithm will perform well when it analyses a new set of machine vibrations, in a previously-unseen context, is simply an assumption and an assumption that can be challenged.
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, the types of damage that build up when a machine is really used in a factory might go beyond what is seen amongst the artificially-created damage recorded in benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-046: Who can say that the types of damage found in the real factory will not be more subtle and hard to detect, or more diverse and thus requiring an algorithm with increased flexibility? Such questions start to introduce uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-047: Differences in the properties of the recorded data might mean that an algorithmic approach does not generalise perfectly, leading to inferior performance when it is applied to a new dataset.
elements.sent-2411-048: Speaking more generally about neural networks, when a practitioner takes an existing algorithm and tries using it on their own data, there is a risk that it does not work.
elements.sent-2411-049: Imagine that the practitioner is left with an algorithm that does not work.
elements.sent-2411-050: What can they do? One of the first things they can try is to modify the hyperparameters of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-051: In order to control the training of a neural network, the user must specify some so-called `hyperparameters', such as the minibatch size (the number of examples used in a single step of training).
elements.sent-2411-052: These hyperparameters control how to generate the training signal that the neural network learns from -- they are used in generating the numbers that incrementally update the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-053: Just like how a mis-tuned radio will not play your favourite radio show, it is possible to mis-tune hyperparameters and end up with a training signal that does not do what you want, i.e., it will not teach the neural network how to do the task.
elements.sent-2411-054: Alongside hyperparameters that modulate the training process, one can consider what we might call `architectural hyperparameters', which control the size and shape of a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-055: Quite commonly, when faced with poor results, a practitioner might make their neural network bigger.
elements.sent-2411-056: The layers used to construct the network could be enlarged or the network could be built by stacking more layers together.
elements.sent-2411-057: After making such changes, the neural network will contain more trainable parameters (numbers used to store information about how to process inputs) and thus have more capacity to learn about the task.
elements.sent-2411-058: If a neural network is not working then quite a natural step is to make it larger by increasing the architectural hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-059: The reason we tweak hyperparameters is that the wrong choice of settings will lead to inferior performance whilst good choices will bring out the best from a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-060: In the case of severe ill-hyperparameterisation, a network might fail to learn at all during training.
elements.sent-2411-061: Returning specifically to bearing fault detection, we noted earlier that the differences appear to be minimal between the top 25 neural networks for detecting damaged bearings.
elements.sent-2411-062: By contrast, larger differences in accuracy can emerge when the hyperparameters of a network are changed.
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, \cite{kim2022effect reports that the accuracy of a bearing fault classifying CNN is cut in half simply as a result of increasing from 6 convolutional layers to 8.}
elements.sent-2411-064: When it comes to getting near-perfect accuracy on bearing fault detection, getting the hyperparameters right might be more important than choosing between neural network architectures.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, although bad hyperparameter settings can result in misery, there is a question of why this matters, because we know that the hyperparameters work on our benchmark data.
elements.sent-2411-066: If the hyperparameters are already configured well, then what exactly is the problem? This is really a question of whether hyperparameters that work well on one dataset will necessarily be successful on another.
elements.sent-2411-067: To hone in on just a single reason why this might not always happen, we can ask whether drastically changing the sampling rate would impact what size the filters in a convolutional network should be.
elements.sent-2411-068: Convolutional filters act like learned patterns that the network recognises at different time points during the input signal.
elements.sent-2411-069: Each pattern is stored in an array of numbers.
elements.sent-2411-070: If we increase the sampling rate used to record a signal that is otherwise kept fixed -- so that more points are used to record the same peaks, troughs and plateaus over time -- then it might be reasonable to expect that the number of points used to record the important patterns (in convolutional filters) also needs to be increased.
elements.sent-2411-071: The main point of this example is to highlight how data properties like the sampling rate might cause a meaningful difference in what hyperparameters are successful on different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-072: In fact, by examining the performance of neural networks on seven benchmark datasets, one important contribution of our work will be to show that hyperparameters working on one dataset sometimes stop working when changing to another dataset.
elements.sent-2411-073: Another question is whether some hyperparameters can be changed without affecting the accuracy of the neural network on a specific dataset.
elements.sent-2411-074: Of course, it is possible to imagine that there is a miniscule `sweet spot' of hyperparameters, a specific combination of numbers which is the only one that works.
elements.sent-2411-075: The reason this seems unlikely is that there is no obvious explanation for how the authors of successful algorithms were able to find this one-and-only sweet spot.
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, we instead expect that at least some of the hyperparameters can be modified without disastrous consequences for the accuracy of the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-077: Having more options in this way, it would be easier for an author to find a successful combination of hyperparameters without performing a rigorous search.
elements.sent-2411-078: Architectural hyperparameters like the number of layers in a network affect how complicated it is to process each input.
elements.sent-2411-079: Making the network smaller reduces the number of internal parameters it has, meaning that the computer needs to perform fewer arithmetic operations.
elements.sent-2411-080: This has a knock-on effect that impacts, for example, how long it takes to train the network.
elements.sent-2411-081: Smaller networks train faster and are quicker to generate predictions on new data.
elements.sent-2411-082: If some architectural hyperparameters can be reduced without harming the accuracy, it would be good to know this.
elements.sent-2411-083: Practitioners would benefit from knowing that they can make the network smaller and thus faster to train and use.
elements.sent-2411-084: Moreover, in real-world settings, there is the possibility of `embedding' fault detection algorithms, so that they run on small devices situated in and amongst the machines they analyse rather than running on a user's laptop.
elements.sent-2411-085: Embedded processing skips the step of extracting data from the sensors and communicating it to a central location (like a laptop), and removes the need to keep a laptop or computer running continuously in order to process the data.
elements.sent-2411-086: An additional benefit of small networks is that they can be run on smaller and cheaper embedded devices.
elements.sent-2411-087: Clearly, there is real value to understanding the hyperparameters of a network, to see which hyperparameters can and cannot be changed when processing a given dataset.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this vein, we have worked to understand the architectural hyperparameters of a network capable of state-of-the-art results on fault detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-089: Of course, there must be limits to how much we can shrink a network or otherwise manipulate its hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-090: Some changes will be too drastic to work and they will make the neural network less able to detect bearing faults.
elements.sent-2411-091: Another line of our research is to study which hyperparameters are crucial and require the most careful handling.
elements.sent-2411-092: We wish to understand how much impact different changes will have and be able to state the relative importance of the hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-093: Re-examining the wide-kernel network presented in ~\cite{zhang2017new,hoogen2020improved , we distil new explanations of how the network's hyperparameters impact its accuracy at classifying bearing fault signals.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Datasets have different properties, one reason why hyperparameters might need (re)tuning on new data even though they were already working on benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the field of bearing vibration, research focuses on sensor recordings stored in `time series', so called because they consist of a series of sequential measurements of some variable over time.
elements.sent-2411-096: The sampling rate is one of the key data properties when discussing time series.
elements.sent-2411-097: With a sampling rate of 12kHz, the movement of a vibrating machine is measured every 0.00008333 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-098: The sampling rate determines how many measurements are made per second and conversely how far apart in time each individual measurement point is.
elements.sent-2411-099: The sampling rate is documented for most benchmark datasets and we can see that it varies.
elements.sent-2411-100: One thread of our research will be to find out if changes to the sampling rate have an impact on the accuracy of a network and on which hyperparameter settings are best.
elements.sent-2411-101: The sampling rate affects the dataset in multiple ways.
elements.sent-2411-102: It affects what frequency bands can be detected, with half the sampling rate (also known as the `Nyquist frequency') providing the upper limit of what frequencies can be captured.
elements.sent-2411-103: An increased sampling rate also means that more numbers are used to record a vibrating machine, implying that the neural network needs to do more computation in order to process the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-104: One open question is how much the highest frequencies in vibration recordings are used by neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-105: This is interesting because if the highest frequencies are not needed, then storing and processing the recordings could be made more efficient by reducing the sampling rate.
elements.sent-2411-106: In fact, if high frequencies are unnecessary for fault detection then removing them could be a form of noise filtering.
elements.sent-2411-107: This is not implausible, since many signals resulting from vibrations (like from musical instruments) have an overtone series, implying that there is redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-108: As noted in \cite{lacey2008vibration , vibrations in bearing elements are transmitted to the sensors through the rest of the machine, which also consists of multiple components that themselves vibrate.}
elements.sent-2411-109: This potentially leads to irrelevant or misleading information in the recorded signal, and if some frequency bands are just noise then it could be beneficial to remove them.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our research looks at how changes in the sampling frequency, for example when moving to one dataset to another one, affect neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-111: We ask whether filtering the irrelevant frequencies be helpful, for example by increasing the size of the `sweet spot' of hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-112: How many layers is enough in a neural network? Why do we set other hyperparameters in the way that we do? Although there are rules to follow when tuning some hyperparameters like the minibatch size (e.g., ``set it to the largest value that will still allow the minibatch to fit into GPU memory'') in other cases we can look at a neural network and find that the hyperparameter settings seem totally arbitrary.
elements.sent-2411-113: To pick just a pair of fault detection papers at random, we find that \cite{eren2017bearing uses 20 hidden units in fully-connected layers whilst \cite{8756423 uses 64 instead.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: As is typical in the field, neither author noted why they chose the particular numbers they use and the reader is left with no explanation of why there is a difference.
elements.sent-2411-115: If there is ever a need to adapt these hyperparameters when working with new datasets, future users could quite justifiably feel lost.
elements.sent-2411-116: What users in fact want is a simple, explainable process for setting the hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-117: The terms ``Explainability'' and ``Explainable AI (XAI)'' tie together several strands of research aimed at clarifying how the inputs to an algorithm relate to the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-118: With complex statistical models such as neural networks the inner workings are not understandable and we must rely upon specialised techniques to illuminate why particular outputs were chosen by the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-119: This is a large topic and there are many surveys of the work done in this research area, such as \cite{SAEED2023110273 for example.}
elements.sent-2411-120: What XAI aims to do is to cast a light on obscure and hidden relationships that exist in the algorithm and which link the inputs to the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-121: In many real-world domains such as bearing fault detection, algorithms need to learn nonlinear mathematical functions in order to approximate the complex interactions between multiple factors seen in observed data.
elements.sent-2411-122: Returning now to our own focus, we want to do something similar with hyperparameters in order to better understand how they propel some networks to near-perfect accuracy and other networks to near-total failure.
elements.sent-2411-123: As we have noted, neural networks are clearly powerful and numerous in bearing fault classification but they are also poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-124: By adapting techniques from XAI, we aim to build a bridge between empirical results and more general insights about how to tune hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-125: The present paper re-examines and then extends upon the results from several pieces of our own past work.
elements.sent-2411-126: In addition to providing new insights on past results, this present study presents new experiments with resampling and filtering of the data, allowing us to investigate how the properties of the data affect hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-127: To summarise the intention of the research: we already have neural network architectures for bearing fault detection (\cite{neupane2024data gives many examples) but their success relies on hyperparameters which are poorly understood.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Hyperparameters become especially important when we remember that the users of a neural network will not want to take it and apply it to a benchmark dataset.
elements.sent-2411-129: For the network to be any use at all, it must be possible for someone who is not the developer to take it and apply it themselves to novel datasets.
elements.sent-2411-130: To do so, they need some idea of how to decide the hyperparameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-131: However, the hyperparameters might as well be written in a cypher -- there is no intuitive way to know that 64 convolutional filters works but a rate of 128 does not.
elements.sent-2411-133: Engineers in a factory might have different machinery and types of bearings from what was used in benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-134: They may have restrictions about where the sensors can be placed, and even what sensors they can use to record vibrations.
elements.sent-2411-135: Together, the specifics of a factory's machinery, bearing types, sensor availability and sensor placement will conspire to make a unique dataset for each engineer.
elements.sent-2411-136: As already noted, the choice of algorithm will not impact performance that much on benchmark datasets (see~\cite{neupane2024data ), but the performance drop as a result of trying out a new dataset might be noticeable.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Therefore, we take an existing neural network architecture which is capable of achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks, and enrich it by presenting a detailed analysis of the hyperparameter decisions presented to the user.
elements.sent-2411-138: We use a cocktail of multiple methods to explain the impact of hyperparameters on the wide-kernel CNN's accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-139: We summarise results from past work in order to see the average performance level when specific hyperparameter values are carried across seven datasets.
elements.sent-2411-140: We also harvest new results from experiments on manipulated versions of the data.
elements.sent-2411-141: We quantify the `feature importance' -- the extent to which different variables contribute to an outcome -- of different hyperparameters and how it changes when processing either resampled or filtered copies of a dataset.
elements.sent-2411-142: This lets us find out how relevant dataset properties like the sampling rate are to deciding what hyperparameters to use.
elements.sent-2411-143: We also undertake separate additional experiments with two other architectures (besides the wide-kernel CNN) in order to round out the generality of our basic premise.
elements.sent-2411-144: The key contributions of this work are:.
elements.sent-2411-145: We show that hyperparameters are important across multiple types of neural networks rather than a single network type.
elements.sent-2411-146: Past results and new experiments with additional neural network architectures confirm this.
elements.sent-2411-147: We show that hyperparameters affect how accurate a neural network is (the majority of our work specifically investigates a wide-kernel CNN architecture).
elements.sent-2411-148: Fusing information from 7 datasets to analyse the wide-kernel architecture, we show that hyperparameters that are successful on one dataset are not necessarily successful on another dataset.
elements.sent-2411-149: We also highlight that not all hyperparameters are equally important.
elements.sent-2411-150: We propose that properties of the dataset, such as sampling rate, impact the optimal values of hyperparameters, which leads us to run experiments with manipulated versions of the time series data.
elements.sent-2411-151: After fusing the information from multiple different experiments, we find that due to redundancy in the signal, very high frequencies are not needed for successful bearing fault detection.
elements.sent-2411-152: We give general guidelines for how to set the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel CNN when transitioning to new data.
elements.sent-2411-153: In this section, we review fault detection for the rotating machines seen in industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-154: We also provide an overview of deep learning (DL) in the realm of time series analysis using sensor data, emphasising the design and training of convolutional neural networks (CNN).
elements.sent-2411-155: Additionally, given that this research concentrates on the architectural hyperparameters of a wide-kernel network, we discuss studies related to hyperparameter optimisation.
elements.sent-2411-156: Detecting faults in rotating industrial machinery is essential to avoid breakdowns~\cite{motor1985report .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Fault detection data are typically collected from sensors that measure vibrations, represented as time series data.
elements.sent-2411-158: Initially, fault detection relied on physics-based models that required an understanding of the underlying mechanisms that generate and propagate vibrations~\cite{yin2014data .}
elements.sent-2411-159: However, these models struggled to adapt to changing environments and increasing data complexity.
elements.sent-2411-160: The advent of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and data-driven analysis techniques has revolutionised fault detection methods, enabling a more intelligent and automated approach~\cite{8756423,zhao2019deep:b .}
elements.sent-2411-161: These advances eliminate the need for in-depth technical knowledge of industrial machinery, allowing for automated processing and adaptation to changing operational environments.
elements.sent-2411-162: For example, machine learning approaches such as K-NN~\cite{pandya2013fault,6967842 , Random Forest~\cite{7974734 , and SVM~\cite{santos2015svm,you2014wpd,huang2011support,konar2011bearing have been applied for fault detection.}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: However, these methods require extensive feature extraction, a time-consuming process that has to be fine-tuned towards the type of data used.
elements.sent-2411-164: With the development of deep learning techniques, these steps were no longer necessary since deep learning techniques are able to automatically extract features from raw data, simplifying the otherwise complex feature extraction process.
elements.sent-2411-165: Initially, multilayer perceptrons (consisting of computational units arranged into simple layers where every unit in one layer connects to every unit in the next layer) were used, but their limited depth due to computational constraints shifted the focus to other architectures such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs), which can more effectively model temporal dependencies~\cite{malhotra2016lstm,9391030 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: An RNN processes sequences of data one step at a time, passing information along internally to keep track of what it has seen.
elements.sent-2411-167: The computational units in an RNN are arranged differently than for an MLP, since the RNN is formed from small blocks suited for processing sequences one step at a time whilst the layers of an MLP must process the entire input sequence in a single vast computation.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, RNN networks are less suited for time series data that appear as especially long sequences, which is common in sensor data sampled at a high frequency (e.g., mechanical vibrations or electrical currents).
elements.sent-2411-169: The main reason for this relates to the storage of long-term dependencies, also resulting in increased computational needs when the time series is of high resolution.
elements.sent-2411-170: On the other hand, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including the one-dimensional version for processing time series gained popularity, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in fault detection, especially with wide-kernel designs in the first convolutional layer~\cite{ince2016real,zhang2017rolling,hoogen2023hyperparams,hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,hoogen2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-171: Convolutions are an efficient alternative to RNNs because they also process sequences as a collection of smaller sub-pieces.
elements.sent-2411-172: Deep Learning draws inspiration from the human brain's ability to combine many simple units into a large system capable of learning difficult tasks~\cite{rosenblatt1958perceptron .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Its strength lies in processing and learning complex data, making it particularly effective for tasks requiring automatic feature extraction and refinement.
elements.sent-2411-174: This capability is crucial when valuable insights can be revealed by recognising patterns and relationships in the data ~\cite{krizhevsky2012imagenet,simonyan2014very .}
elements.sent-2411-175: The fundamental component of a neural network is the artificial neuron, or ``node," which processes multiple data inputs to produce an output.
elements.sent-2411-176: Many nodes can be grouped together into a layer and the term ``deep" in deep learning refers to the inclusion of many layers in neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-177: A simple representation of a neuron \( i \) is given by: \begin{equation}a_i = \sigma\left(\sum_j w_{ij} x_j + b_i\right)\end{equation} Here, \( x_j \) are the inputs, \( w_{ij \) are the weights, \( b_i \) is the bias, and \( \sigma \) is a nonlinear activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Each input is multiplied by a corresponding weight and the resulting values are added together along with an extra bias term.
elements.sent-2411-179: The formula operates somewhat like a recipe that combines different amounts of the various inputs (the \( x_j \) terms) and adds some final garnish (the bias \( b_i \)).
elements.sent-2411-180: The nonlinearity introduced by the activation function is needed in order to handle difficult tasks, for example when similar inputs need different outputs.
elements.sent-2411-181: With layers stacking and interconnecting in complex architectures, neural networks can develop into intricate patterns, which gives them the flexibility and complexity needed to handle difficult tasks like image and speech recognition~\cite{krizhevsky2012imagenet,simonyan2014very,lecun1995convolutional,hannun2014deep .}
elements.sent-2411-182: The development of the multilayer perceptron (MLP) marked the beginning of deep learning, featuring fully connected layers~\cite{rosenblatt1958perceptron,rumelhart1986learning .}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, computational limitations initially restricted the depth of these networks.
elements.sent-2411-184: More advanced architectures were developed to overcome the limitations MLPs had when processing time series.
elements.sent-2411-185: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), particularly long-short-term memory (LSTM) networks, emerged as effective architectures for time series analysis due to their ability to capture temporal dependencies~\cite{malhotra2016lstm,9391030,hannun2014deep .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Despite their success, RNNs are memory-intensive and less suited for long sequence data due to increased training times.
elements.sent-2411-187: RNNs are therefore not appropriate for directly processing raw inputs in many cases, and the undesirable step of pre-processing becomes necessary once again.
elements.sent-2411-188: The initial application of MLPs in time series analysis spanned various domains, including stock prediction~\cite{naeini2010stock , weather forecasting~\cite{abhishek2012weather , and fault detection~\cite{hajnayeb2011application .}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Subsequently, RNNs~\cite{malhotra2016lstm,hannun2014deep and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)~\cite{zhang2017rolling,zhao2019deep:b,s19235300,8756423,yang2015deep demonstrated significant performance improvements in time series tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: CNNs, particularly one-dimensional (1D) CNNs were designed to process raw time series data effectively, leveraging automated feature extraction~\cite{ince2016real,zheng2014time .}
elements.sent-2411-191: These networks are robust against noise in the data and can be trained with relatively small datasets~\cite{zhang2019limited .}
elements.sent-2411-192: For fault detection a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) where all layers are fully connected~\cite{hajnayeb2011application was initially proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Afterwards, the field of fault detection began to utilise convolutional neural networks (CNN)~\cite{8756423,zhao2019deep:b,s19235300,zhang2017rolling due to their notable performance improvements.}
elements.sent-2411-194: CNN methods, when combined with data transformations like spectrograms, have been employed multiple times~\cite{zhao2019deep:a,chen2019intelligent .}
elements.sent-2411-195: However, 1D CNNs are able to process the raw time series data directly (without the need for spectrograms) and integrate automated feature extraction, and are often used in fault detection.
elements.sent-2411-196: These 1D CNNs also tend to be resilient to noise in time series data and can be trained with a small sample size~\cite{zhang2019limited .}
elements.sent-2411-197: Subsequently, numerous refinements for fault detection using convolutions were introduced.
elements.sent-2411-198: These include, but are not limited to, wide-kernel CNNs~\cite{zhang2017new,hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,hoogen2021 (which are used in this work), attention CNNs~\cite{zhao2023fault , periodic CNNs~\cite{periodnet , and CNN autoencoders for unsupervised learning tasks~\cite{CHEN202054 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Each of these networks has unique characteristics regarding complexity, computational requirements, and specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-200: The general mechanics behind CNNs are further discussed in the next paragraph.
elements.sent-2411-201: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a specialised type of neural network that were originally created to handle two-dimensional (2D) data, such as images.
elements.sent-2411-202: Introduced by LeCun in 1989~\cite{lecun1998gradient,lecun1995convolutional , CNNs utilise a feed-forward architecture that applies convolutions instead of general matrix multiplications, making them highly effective for data organised in a grid-like structure.}
elements.sent-2411-203: In contrast to conventional MLPs, CNNs have become essential in areas such as Computer Vision, Image Recognition~\cite{lecun1995convolutional,simonyan2014very , and are increasingly used in time series analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-204: The primary benefits of CNNs compared to other neural networks arise from their use of local receptive fields, weight-sharing, and sub-sampling~\cite{goodfellow2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-205: These characteristics greatly decrease memory usage and computational complexity, thereby improving algorithmic efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-206: Convolutional layers apply filters to convolve input data and this basically allows the network to identify where patterns occur within a large signal.
elements.sent-2411-207: The activation function, commonly a Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU), introduces non-linearity, allowing the network to learn intricate patterns~\cite{gulcehre2016noisy .}
elements.sent-2411-208: After convolution and activation, a pooling layer often shrinks the signal's size in order to decrease the number of parameters of the next layer which helps prevent overfitting and lowers computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-209: Initially, CNNs were less common for time series data due to their 2D nature, requiring conversion of one-dimensional (1D) data into 2D matrices, adding computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-210: This changed with the development of 1D CNNs, which can directly process raw time series data~\cite{kiranyaz2015convolutional,hoogen2021,zhang2017new,zhang2019limited,hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,ince2016real .}
elements.sent-2411-211: These advancements have solidified CNNs as a powerful tool for time series analysis, capable of efficiently handling high-frequency sensor data and extracting valuable features with minimal preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-212: The convolution operation is mathematically expressed as: \begin{equation}y_{i}^{l+1}(j)=k_{i}^{l} * M^{l}(j)+b_{i}^{l},\end{equation} where $b_{i ^{l}$ is the bias, $k_{i ^{l}$ represents the filter weights, and $M^{l (j)$ is the local region of the input in layer $l$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: The weights of the filter act in some ways like a learned pattern, in the sense that the filter reacts most strongly (gives the largest numbers as outputs) when the input correlates with the weights of the filter in addition to being large.
elements.sent-2411-214: The filter applies to little regions of the overall signal with each region being one stride along from the previous one.
elements.sent-2411-215: In the notation above $i$ is a number used to refer to different regions.
elements.sent-2411-216: The most common activation function is ReLU~\cite{agarap2019deeplearningusingrectified , which introduces nonlinearity to the network.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Next, a pooling layer is utilised that allows the CNN to downsample the output of the convolutional layer making it more resource efficient in various applications from image and speech recognition to time series analysis.
elements.sent-2411-218: Past literature furnishes us with several examples of hyperparameter searches: activities in which authors tweak and tune the hyperparameters of a neural network in order to find the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-219: Because many decisions when designing and training neural networks can be called `hyperparameters', previous studies have different degrees of overlap.
elements.sent-2411-220: Working in the field of sentiment analysis, \cite{priyadarshini2021novel used a grid search -- which takes a list of valid values for each hyperparameter individually and then tests out every possible combination of these values -- to optimise the accuracy of their LSTM-CNN network.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Their motivation for using the grid search approach was to guarantee the absolute best performance, which more efficient search methods trade off in favour of increased processing speed.
elements.sent-2411-222: As will be described in Section \ref{sec:method , we also employ a grid search method, although our reason for doing so is that it supports a wider range of follow-on analyses rather than to find a single combination of hyperparameters that is best.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Grid search is fairly common, for example being used by \cite{jana2022cnn to optimise the three training hyperparameters of learning rate, minibatch size and dropout ratio when training a CNN.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The authors of \cite{jana2022cnn used the Talos package to implement their hyperparameter grid search.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Bayesian optimisation is another popular technique to find good hyperparameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-226: When developing a CNN-GRU network to detect different types of activities from wearable fitness tracker-type devices, \cite{gupta2021deep used the Optuna and OptKeras packages to implement their Bayesian optimisation search.}
elements.sent-2411-227: The hyperparameters they tuned included the number of convolutional filters in different layers, and the number of hidden units in the GRU layers.
elements.sent-2411-228: The authors of \cite{kalouris2019improving used SigOpt to perform Bayesian optimisation on a wide range of hyperparameters, from minibatch size and learning rate to the number of fully-connected neurons and the properties of the convolutional layers in their network.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Furthermore, \cite{yi2020automated applied Bayesian optimisation for LSTM-RNNs in highway traffic prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Another popular possibility for performing a hyperparameter search is to use a genetic algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-231: At the beginning of a genetic algorithm, there is a population of candidate solutions which are tested out.
elements.sent-2411-232: The key step is that, after testing out a population of candidates, a new `generation' of candidate solutions is created to replace the old population of candidates, and the new generation is formed by combining and mutating candidates from the previous generation with some preference being given to the candidates that performed best.
elements.sent-2411-233: Such an algorithm is used by \cite{mostafa2020multi to tune hyperparameters such as the number of layers, the number and size of convolutions in a layer, and the number of fully-connected neurons in a CNN for identifying sleep apnea.}
elements.sent-2411-234: A genetic algorithm was used by \cite{zatarain2020hyperparameter to decide a very similar collection of hyperparameters, although in this case the CNN was applied to emotion recognition in smart tutoring.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Beyond the search methods already mentioned, there are more esoteric ideas that are sometimes presented as alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-236: An example of a more uncommon approach is to use orthogonal arrays to decide which hyperparameter combinations to test out.
elements.sent-2411-237: In \cite{zhang2019orthogonal , experiments with RNN and CNN networks on three time series datasets found that a hyperparameter search based on orthogonal arrays got closer to the global maximum level of accuracy for a limited time budget, compared to random search and Bayesian optimisation alternatives.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Although these studies provided valuable insights, their methods and objectives differ from the present work, which seeks a comprehensive view of network performance through a grid search rather than focusing on optimisation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-239: Hence, our approach emphasises an extensive automated grid search, inspired by the work in \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams,hudson2024stay to determine if the most important hyperparameters change as a result of differences in times series properties.}
elements.sent-2411-240: In this way, we attempt to better understand and explain the hyperparameters wide-kernel CNN.
elements.sent-2411-241: In \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams we investigated how choices of the hyperparameters for a specific deep learning architecture (wide-kernel CNN) affect its performance in classifying faults from vibration signals in industrial machines.}
elements.sent-2411-242: This architecture consists of five convolutional layers including an initial `wide' layer with a large kernel size which then feed into a fully-connected classifier.
elements.sent-2411-243: See Section \ref{sec:method:widekernelarchitecture for a detailed description.}
elements.sent-2411-244: We were particularly interested in learning more about how performance changes across the hyperparameter space and so we performed a large-scale analysis, training and testing many versions of the wide-kernel CNN with different configurations (e.g., number of filters, kernel size) on three different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-245: The research used a grid search to explore the hyperparameter space and then presented multiple pieces of follow-on analysis.
elements.sent-2411-246: Looking at how the distribution of test accuracy scores changes as a result of tweaking individual hyperparameters, it was found that the number of filters in the later convolutional layers (3 to 5) has the biggest impact on performance, with an optimal value around 32.
elements.sent-2411-247: The kernel size in the first layer was also found to be important for datasets with a high sampling rate, where larger kernels perform better.
elements.sent-2411-248: Another discovery was that high-performing hyperparameter settings could be unstable, i.e., small changes to hyperparameters can have a large impact on performance, highlighting the need for careful tuning.
elements.sent-2411-249: Overall, this study provided insights into how to configure a wide-kernel CNN for better fault detection in industrial machinery.
elements.sent-2411-250: We also identified interesting areas for future research, such as applying this approach to other signal classification tasks and different neural network architectures.
elements.sent-2411-251: A second study increased the number of benchmark datasets by four and applied 12,960 variations of the wide-kernel CNN to seven different industrial vibration datasets \cite{hudson2024stay .}
elements.sent-2411-252: Once again, we analysed the grid search in order to identify how hyperparameter settings influence the network's performance and how to effectively tune them for new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-253: Compared to the previous study~\cite{hudson2024stay , a more thorough investigation using seven datasets confirmed that the most important hyperparameters for the network's performance are the number of filters in layers 3-5 and the kernel size in the first layer.}
elements.sent-2411-254: New analysis focused on pairwise interactions between hyperparameters, where changing one hyperparameter might require adjusting another one for optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-255: After looking at how hyperparameters influence one another, we suggested a specific order for tuning the hyperparameters to minimise the need to re-tune them later.
elements.sent-2411-256: This would be useful in situations where practitioners want to tune the hyperparameters one-at-a-time sequentially rather than using a full grid search.
elements.sent-2411-257: Tuning the hyperparameters in our suggested order leads to better performance compared to random or reverse order.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our research method consists of several parts which feed into each other.
elements.sent-2411-259: In this section, we step through each piece of the method and describe it in detail.
elements.sent-2411-260: Starting out, we describe seven different benchmark datasets that we use to train and test neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-261: Different types of fault, machinery and recording set-up are possible in fault detection and we therefore expect that datasets can differ from one another.
elements.sent-2411-262: We want to account for variations in the data and so we assembled a selection of seven benchmarks to train and test out CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-263: The properties of the benchmark datasets are described in detail below.
elements.sent-2411-264: Next, our method splits into two main structural components based on which architectures we study.
elements.sent-2411-265: Previous work in \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams and \cite{hudson2024stay looked at the consequences of tuning the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel CNN but not other kinds of architecture.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: We devote a short section to the impact of hyperparameter tuning on two other architectures with the goal of showing more generally that hyperparameters matter for bearing fault classification, at least when using neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-267: We introduce two popular types of architecture that are used in this short section as alternatives to a wide-kernel CNN.
elements.sent-2411-268: Additionally, we describe how we generate results by sampling different hyperparameter values from a range of possibilities and then training and testing networks on the seven benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-269: The second structural component and the majority of the research however focuses on the wide-kernel CNN and aims to investigate how much dataset properties affect which hyperparameter settings are best.
elements.sent-2411-270: We describe the wide-kernel CNN architecture used in our work and its architectural hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-271: Training and testing on seven benchmark datasets gives an indication of how differences in the data can have consequences for hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-272: We start off with a grid search which is an exhaustive exploration of different combinations of hyperparameters and for each combination we train and test a CNN on benchmark data.
elements.sent-2411-273: We repeat the grid search seven times in order to include all the benchmark datasets mentioned earlier.
elements.sent-2411-274: In addition to accepting the benchmark datasets as-is, we also want to get into a position to see what happens when we manipulate the dataset properties ourselves.
elements.sent-2411-275: By manipulating the datasets, we gain fine-grained control over factors that might explain why some hyperparameter settings result in high accuracy while others perform badly.
elements.sent-2411-276: In concrete terms, we edit the sampling frequency of the CWRU dataset by resampling it and we also cut out high frequencies by filtering.
elements.sent-2411-277: Both types of data manipulation are described below.

elements.sent-2411-279: Collating these grid searches, we can compare how hyperparameters behave in response to different levels of resampling or filtering.
elements.sent-2411-280: At this point, we have several different types of architecture being tested out on different datasets; for clarity, we illustrate the overall flow of the various steps in Figure \ref{fig:workflow .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Ultimately we wish to extract useful insights into how to tune hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-282: There are actually several related analyses based on the outputs of the grid searches, and we go over each one in turn.
elements.sent-2411-283: Correlations are used to see how much hyperparameter settings generalise across datasets.
elements.sent-2411-284: Feature importance is used to see which hyperparameters matter the most.
elements.sent-2411-285: We have two approaches, the first of which is based on Shapley values and the second of which is based on the d-dimensional earth mover's score.
