elements.subsection-2411-013: Method Pt. 1 -- Experiments on Alternatives to Wide-Kernel CNN Architecture
elements.sent-2411-363: A wide-kernel CNN architecture takes centre stage in our research however we also want to say that hyperparameters are an important and unavoidable decision for other neural networks too.
elements.sent-2411-364: Therefore, in addition to the wide-kernel CNN which is our focus we also perform limited experiments on two additional architectures.
elements.sent-2411-365: By encompassing more than one architectural style, our work has a broader basis on which more general conclusions can be built.
elements.sent-2411-366: The first additional architecture uses ``long- short-term memory'' (LSTM) to digest the signal as a sequence of smaller pieces.
elements.sent-2411-367: The second additional architecture, a ``transformer'', also consumes its inputs as a sequence.
elements.sent-2411-368: Both architectures boast a good record of accomplishing sequence processing tasks and can be adapted to work on time series.
elements.sent-2411-369: Our goals for the LSTM and transformer architectures are not the same as those we have for the wide-kernel CNN.
elements.sent-2411-370: With the wide-kernel CNN, our intention is to weigh up the importance of different hyperparameters and familiarise ourselves with each hyperparameter in detail.
elements.sent-2411-371: Our results do not generalise to other architectures which have their own distinct sets of hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-372: The extra LSTM and transformer architectures are not included to allow for a detailed analysis but simply to provide evidence that hyperparameter tuning has important consequences for a wide range of state-of-the-art neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-373: We want to show that the dangers of ill-hyperparameterisation are not a consequence of choosing the wide-kernel architecture in particular.
elements.sent-2411-374: Following the example of \cite{chen2021bearing , we consider a bearing fault classifier built around Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) layers.}
elements.sent-2411-375: The central idea of an LSTM layer is that it passes information forward between steps when processing a sequence stepwise -- making it possible to `remember' things seen earlier -- and yet it also allows the internal flow of information to be flushed out \cite{hochreiter1997long .}
elements.sent-2411-376: Strategically flushing out the internal information deals with the problems of vanishing and exploding gradients that previous kinds of recurrent network suffered from, with the upshot being that LSTMs are more successful at handling longer sequences and performing a wide range of tasks.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.6\textwidth]{bin/cnn_lstm.jpg} \caption{Architecture of the used LSTM network, inspired by~\cite{chen2021bearing}. The network utilises two convolutional paths at different scales which are multiplied together and fed into the main LSTM. } \label{fig:lstm_architecture}
elements.sent-2411-377: Like \cite{chen2021bearing , we situate LSTM layers in an architecture that can be imagined as a structure with three main chambers.}
elements.sent-2411-378: The first two components are CNNs which proceed in parallel and then they both pass into the third main component which is the LSTM itself.
elements.sent-2411-379: Two CNNs are used in order to achieve a ``multi-scale'' effect.
elements.sent-2411-380: A CNN containing smaller kernels gives prominence to brief or high-frequency vibrations in the signal.
elements.sent-2411-381: Features of the signal that develop more slowly are emphasised in a second CNN consisting of larger kernels.
elements.sent-2411-382: The patterns that emerge from both CNNs then proceed into the LSTM.
elements.sent-2411-383: Crucially, the patterns coming from the CNNs take on the form of a sequence, in which each step summarises a period of time from the raw signal.
elements.sent-2411-384: This is needed because the LSTM layer would be overwhelmed by the sheer number of measurement points in the raw signal without any summarisation.
elements.sent-2411-385: The LSTM component takes the shape of a pair of layers which run along the length of the input sequence and ultimately exit to a vector of numbers which captures the key details of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-386: At the end of the LSTM, fully connected layers head onwards to arrive at a final prediction of what type of fault is present in the machinery.
elements.sent-2411-387: The architecture is visualised in Figure \ref{fig:lstm_architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-388: We also consider a transformer architecture, following the example set by \cite{zhao2023fault for bearing fault analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Transformers use a so-called `attention' mechanism which allows them to process each step in a sequence by pooling information from other steps \cite{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-390: The innovation of using attention provides an efficient alternative to recurrent connections, and the booming popularity of transformers in applications like ChatGPT testifies to their efficacy.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=.6\textwidth]{bin/faultformer.jpg} \caption{Architecture of the used transformer network, inspired by~\cite{zhao2023fault}. The network utilises convolutional layers that feed into transformer layers. } \label{fig:transformer_architecture}
elements.sent-2411-391: Much like the LSTM architecture, the transformer would also be quickly overwhelmed by the number of time steps in the raw vibration signal.
elements.sent-2411-392: Therefore, the network we use (shown in Figure \ref{fig:transformer_architecture ) receives incoming signals in a CNN component which leads to a compact sequence that then passes through to the transformer component.}
elements.sent-2411-393: The CNN has a simple structure arranged into two layers and after passing though it the raw input signal shrinks to a sequence of roughly 1/16th the length.
elements.sent-2411-394: The transformer section of the architecture is larger and consists of 4 layers by default.
elements.sent-2411-395: When exiting the transformer layers we still have a sequence of vectors.
elements.sent-2411-396: All the pieces of the sequence need to come together into a single vector, something that is achieved using a global average pooling layer.
elements.sent-2411-397: Following the pooling layer it is possible to proceed into a single fully-connected layer which brings us to the final prediction of the network.
elements.sent-2411-398: When studying the LSTM and transformer networks we restrict ourselves to drawing some generic conclusions about the impact of (mis)tuning hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-399: As such, we do not find it necessary to perform a full grid search, but instead to rely on a reduced sample from a grid of possibilities.
elements.sent-2411-400: We select 100 configurations uniformly at random from a larger collection of possible hyperparameter combinations.
elements.sent-2411-401: For the LSTM-based neural network, the values we consider for each hyperparameter are shown in Table \ref{tab:hyperparametersSummaryLSTM .}
elements.sent-2411-402: The middle value for each hyperparameter is based on what it is set to by default in the code published alongside \cite{chen2021bearing .}
elements.sent-2411-403: The other two options simply expand or shrink the amount by a factor of four.
elements.sent-2411-404: From the space of possibilities defined in Table \ref{tab:hyperparametersSummaryLSTM , we take 100 random samples.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Hyperparameter values considered for the LSTM network.\label{tab:hyperparametersSummaryLSTM}} \begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}} Hyperparameter Value Domain Kernel size shallow network layer 1 \{5, 20, 80\} Filters shallow network layer 1 \{13, 50, 200\} Kernel size shallow network layer 2 Always half of layer 1 value Filters shallow network layer 2 \{8, 30, 120\} Kernel size deep network layer 1 \{2, 6, 24\} Filters deep network layer 1 \{13, 50, 200\} Kernel size deep network layer 2 Always equal to layer 1 value Filters deep network layer 2 \{10, 40, 160\} Kernel size deep network layer 3 Always equal to layer 1 value Filters deep network layer 3 \{8, 30, 120\} Kernel size deep network layer 4 Always equal to layer 1 value Filters deep network layer 4 Always equal to filters in layer 2 of the shallow network Hidden units LSTM layer 1 \{15, 60, 240\} Hidden units LSTM layer 2 \{15, 60, 240\} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-405: The transformer hyperparameters are also confined to a limited set of possibilities, which are shown in Table \ref{tab:hyperparametersSummaryTransformer .}
elements.sent-2411-406: From these we choose a sample of 100 combinations of values.
elements.sent-2411-407: Like with the LSTM, our choice of default options is inspired by the code published by the authors (alongside \cite{zhao2023fault ).}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Hyperparameter values considered for the transformer network.\label{tab:hyperparametersSummaryTransformer}} \begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}} Hyperparameter Value Domain Kernel size CNN layer 1 \{4, 16, 64\} Filters CNN layer 1 \{1, 4, 16\} Kernel size CNN layer 2 \{4, 16, 64\} Filters CNN layer 2 \{2, 8, 32\} Hidden units in transformer layers \{64, 256, 1024\} Attention heads in transformer layers \{8, 32, 128\} Number of transformer layers \{1, 4, 16\} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-408: Our analysis of the LSTM and transformer architectures asks how much the accuracy of a network changes when its hyperparameters change.
elements.sent-2411-409: The evidence considered is a sample of 100 LSTMs and 100 transformers chosen at random from the range of hyperparameter options, with each network trained and evaluated on seven benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-410: Based on their hyperparameters, some of the 200 networks perform better and some perform worse.
elements.sent-2411-411: To give an overview of the results, we present to the reader a summary of the distribution of accuracy scores.
elements.sent-2411-412: We report the distribution of test accuracy -- the spread of how well the networks performed -- as follows.
elements.sent-2411-413: First, for the LSTM we state the minimum, 25th percentile, median, 75th percentile and maximum accuracy achieved after training and testing on CWRU data.
elements.sent-2411-414: We then state what values the LSTM reached on the Gearbox data for the minimum, 25th percentile, median, 75th percentile and maximum (also known as Tukey's five-number summary).
elements.sent-2411-415: The results proceed to give the corresponding values for all the other benchmark datasets and when using the transformer instead.
elements.sent-2411-416: In total, there are 5 values describing how 2 architectures did on 7 different datasets, with the goal being to give an idea of the spread of the data and quickly clarifies how much the architectures are affected by their hyperparameters and by changes in the data.
elements.sent-2411-417: We are also curious how well the default hyperparameter values used in past literature fare.
elements.sent-2411-418: Therefore we also report the accuracy obtained when using the default settings.
elements.subsection-2411-014: Method Pt. 2 -- Focusing on the Wide-Kernel CNN Architecture
elements.sent-2411-419: Next we leave behind the LSTM and transformer architecture and arrive at the main body of the research, which is to explain how to set the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel CNN architecture.
elements.sent-2411-420: Previous work demonstrated excellent performance in bearing fault detection by employing a wide-kernel in the first convolutional layer followed by smaller kernels in subsequent layers~\cite{hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,zhang2017new,hoogen2021,hoogen2023hyperparams .}
elements.sent-2411-421: This wide-kernel CNN does not only perform well but is also particularly suitable for fault detection tasks due to its reasonable size in terms of convolutional layers and its capability to process raw time series directly.
elements.sent-2411-422: Our architecture contains two convolutional layers, each utilising several combinations of stride, kernel size and filters.
elements.sent-2411-423: After that, a convolutional unit (see Figure~\ref{fig:architecture ) is repeated three times, resulting in a total network of five convolutional layers.}
elements.sent-2411-424: The three convolutional units all have the same hyperparameter combination in this study, relying on the observation that the deeper layers of the network should have the same properties for optimal learning, as described in previous work~\cite{hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,hoogen2021,zhang2017new .}
elements.sent-2411-425: After each convolutional layer, the network utilises local average pooling to decrease the vector size of the convolutional output with length $T$ divided by two, resulting in a pooled output length of $\frac{T {2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-426: At last, the reduced output from the convolutional layers is fed to two fully connected layers and the last one functions as the classification layer.
elements.sent-2411-427: Figure~\ref{fig:architecture shows the overall network architecture with two exemplary time series as input.}
elements.sent-2411-428: We originally chose to use a wide-kernel CNN architecture as the focus of our hyperparameter investigation because it had already been achieved near-perfect accuracy on the two bearing fault detection datasets (CWRU and Paderborn) in prior work~\cite{zhang2017new,hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,hoogen2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-429: Its performance on these benchmarks indicated to us that it is well-suited for fault detection tasks and that a more complicated network would not necessarily give extra benefit.
elements.sent-2411-430: Furthermore, the architecture is lightweight in terms of computational resources and memory usage, which allows it to have a wider range of uses, in particular opening up the possibility of edge computing in the context of Industry 4.0.
elements.sent-2411-431: Moreover, the modest depth of the wide-kernel architecture, compared to other state-of-the-art CNN architectures~\cite{periodnet, zhao2023fault,qiang2023intelligent,CHEN202054, chen2021bearing, zhou2024faultformer , lends itself well for an extensive grid search of the hyperparameters due to the relatively low number of hyperparameters.}
elements.sent-2411-432: In addition to the network's architecture, some additional refinements improve performance and computation time.
elements.sent-2411-433: These remained the same for every combination that was tested.
elements.sent-2411-434: First of all, between convolutional layers, a batch normalisation layer is added to speed up the training process.
elements.sent-2411-435: Second, we used the Adam stochastic optimiser.
elements.sent-2411-436: Adam is an optimisation algorithm that leverages the strength of adaptive learning rates for each individual parameter and is well-suited for networks that analyse high dimensional input and have a lot of trainable parameters, since it is memory-light and computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-437: Furthermore, according to~\cite{kingma2014adam , Adam is very effective with noisy signals.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=.6\textwidth]{bin/newest_wkcnn.jpg} \caption{Architecture of the used 1D wide-kernel CNN, inspired by~\cite{hoogen2020improvedWDCNN,hoogen2023hyperparams}. The network utilises two convolutional layers followed by a convolutional unit (in grey), which is repeated 3 times. } \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-438: Each hyperparameter of a neural network is a decision and changing any one of them leads to a new configuration for the network, potentially leading to a different accuracy score on the task being attempted.
elements.sent-2411-439: From any given starting point there are as many directions for the configuration of the network to go as there are hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-440: By imposing lower and upper limits on what values each hyperparameter can take, it is possible to chart out a finite hyperparameter space for exploration.
elements.sent-2411-441: In the ensuing section of this paper, we describe the hyperparameter spaces used in our different experiments and how we explore them.
elements.sent-2411-442: Earlier work on the wide-kernel CNN identified seven architectural hyperparameters \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams .}
elements.sent-2411-443: These were explored using a grid search strategy in which each hyperparameter is restricted to a limited set of values and every possible way of combining these is tested.
elements.sent-2411-444: For example, every kernel size in the first layer is tried out with every stride in layer 1, and each of these combinations is tried out with every kernel size in layer 2, etc. Since they differ from one another, the set of options tried for any particular hyperparameter is not the same as for other hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-446: Looking at all the mixtures that can be created, there are $5 \times 3 \times 6 \times 2 \times 6\times 2\times 6 = 12960$ combinations included in the grid search.
elements.sent-2411-447: Previous work also found that three hyperparameters in particular are important: kernel size in the first layer, number of filters in the first layer and number of filters in layers 3-5 (these are indicated in italics in \ref{tab:hyperparametersSummary ) \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams,hudson2024stay .}}
elements.sent-2411-448: We use these hyperparameters as a basis for new grid searches.
elements.sent-2411-449: The reason we are performing new grid searches is that we want to find out more about the impact of manipulating the properties of the data by filtering or resampling.
elements.sent-2411-450: Our approach to filtering and resampling the data is described in the next subsections.
elements.sent-2411-451: By manipulating the dataset we create new versions of it.
elements.sent-2411-452: On each version, we perform a grid search considering the values shown in Table \ref{tab:hyperparametersSummary .}
elements.sent-2411-453: The data manipulation strategies and the goals of performing them are explained in more detail in the next subsection.
elements.sent-2411-454: In summary, we have a grid search performed over seven hyperparameters which tests the accuracy of the network on seven benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-455: Additionally, as will be explained in a moment, we have multiple smaller grid searches which apply to modified versions of the data.
elements.sent-2411-456: These smaller searches operate over three hyperparameters.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Architectural hyperparameters with the values they take in grid search.\label{tab:hyperparametersSummary}} \begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}} Hyperparameter Value Domain Kernel size layer 1 \{16, 32, 64, 128, 256\} Stride layer 1 \{4, 8, 16\} Filters layer 1 \{8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256\} Kernel size layer 2 \{3, 6\} Filters layer 2 \{8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256\} Kernel size layers 3-5 \{3, 6\} Filters layers 3-5 \{8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256\} \end{tabular}
elements.subsubsection-2411-015: Analysis Techniques on Seven Benchmark Datasets
elements.sent-2411-457: In order to flesh out certain points from the work reported earlier in \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams, hudson2024stay , we take the results from a grid search over seven hyperparameters applied to seven benchmark datasets and present them in new ways, as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-458: Descriptive Analysis: If possible, we would like to recommend effective default values for the seven architectural hyperparameters of our wide-kernel CNN architecture.
elements.sent-2411-459: Recommendations would be easy to make if we find that a single value performs best across all the seven benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-460: Therefore, we present a simple table that shows the average accuracy obtained on each dataset when using each hyperparameter value.
elements.sent-2411-461: The table compares different options for a hyperparameter, showing if there is a generally best option or if instead there is no obvious default value that performs well on all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-462: Influence between Important Hyperparameters: Part of the work of \cite{hudson2024stay was to attribute importance scores to the hyperparameters after testing out hyperparameter configurations on seven benchmark datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-463: These importance scores represented the extent to which a hyperparameter impacts the accuracy of the wide-kernel CNN.
elements.sent-2411-464: In particular, the results identified three especially important hyperparameters: the number of filters in the first layer, the kernel size in the first layer, and the number of filters in layers 3-5.
elements.sent-2411-465: In the aforementioned past work there was also an analysis of the influence each hyperparameter on other hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-466: The `influence' of A on B was interpreted as the likelihood that the optimal value for B will change as a result of tuning A.
elements.sent-2411-467: In other words, A is influential on B if tuning A is likely to mean that B needs re-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-468: When someone tunes a network, they tweak the options available to them until they find hyperparameter settings that result in better accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-469: One approach to the problem is to focus on a single choice -- for example the number of convolutional filters used in the first layer of the network -- and modify this value without changing anything else.
elements.sent-2411-470: We call this approach tuning a hyperparameter `individually'.
elements.sent-2411-471: Tuning individually reduces the number of possibilities, perhaps to testing out a handful of values the hyperparameter might reasonably take.
elements.sent-2411-472: After trying the possibilities the best performer will be retained.
elements.sent-2411-473: An underlying assumption of individually tuning hyperparameters is that there is a definite starting point.
elements.sent-2411-474: All of the other hyperparameters must be given a fixed value at the beginning of the process.
elements.sent-2411-475: The results from individual tuning are therefore contingent on there being a starting point and indeed we may find that a different starting point leads to a different optimal value for the hyperparameter being tuned.
elements.sent-2411-476: Even if a hyperparameter has been given the `best' value through individually tuning it, we may find that the best value changes when another hyperparameter has been edited.
elements.sent-2411-477: In which case, the original hyperparameter needs to be re-tuned to reflect a new starting point.
elements.sent-2411-478: It is not clear whether changing one hyperparameter will have any impact on another.
elements.sent-2411-479: It is possible for hyperparameters to be largely independent so that we can modify one of them freely without it affecting which value is optimal for the other hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-480: Alternatively, they could be closely linked and it might be best to tune them so they always change together.
elements.sent-2411-481: In our research we consider the derived question of whether tuning one hyperparameter individually has an impact on the outcome from tuning another hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-482: The answer will be a probability because we need to summarise what happens across a large range of starting points.
elements.sent-2411-483: We have seven hyperparameters and each way of setting them is a different starting point.
elements.sent-2411-484: We therefore revisit the notion of `influence' used in \cite{hudson2024stay and computed by Algorithm \ref{alg:influence .}}
elements.sent-2411-485: This fairly straightforward method to quantify influence considers every combination of values in a grid search as a starting point.
elements.sent-2411-486: From each starting point the algorithm tests the influence of individually tuning one hyperparameter (let us call it `A') on another hyperparameter (`B').
elements.sent-2411-487: First, B is tuned, then A is tuned and subsequently B is re-tuned to see if the optimal value has changed.
elements.sent-2411-488: If re-tuning changes the value of B then the algorithm increments a running count.
elements.sent-2411-489: At the end, the algorithm returns the final value of the running count divided by the number of starting points that were tested.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Compute the influence of $A$ on $B$}\label{alg:influence} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE trialCount $\gets$ 0 \STATE differenceCount $\gets$ 0 \FOR{each configuration $\mathbf{c} \in (\mathbf{c_1}, \mathbf{c_2}, ..., \mathbf{c_{12960}})$} \STATE $B_{tuned} \gets$ tune($\mathbf{c}$, $B$) \STATE $\mathbf{c'} \gets \mathbf{c}$ \STATE $\mathbf{c'}[A] \gets$ tune($\mathbf{c'}$, $A$) \STATE $B_{re-tuned} \gets$ tune($\mathbf{c'}$, $B$) \STATE trialCount $\gets$ trialCount $+ 1$ \IF{$B_{tuned} \neq B_{re-tuned}$} \STATE differenceCount $\gets$ differenceCount $+ 1$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR each \RETURN differenceCount $/$ trialCount \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-490: When reporting these influence scores, \cite{hudson2024stay provided a visualisation to show the influence each hyperparameter has on one another.}
elements.sent-2411-491: In the current paper, we repeat this procedure but consider what happens when only the three most important hyperparameters are tuned and the other hyperparameters are held to their default values (as defined in \cite{hoogen2020improved ).}
elements.sent-2411-492: We show this new information since it allows us to focus on strategies that allow for faster and more effective tuning.
elements.sent-2411-493: Excluding hyperparameters that have low impact on the accuracy of the CNN makes it possible to cut down on computation.
elements.sent-2411-494: When tuning the first layer of the wide-kernel CNN we often find that the largest kernel size is best, but sometimes a small kernel is best and sometimes it does not matter.
elements.sent-2411-495: It depends what benchmark dataset is being used and therefore seems to be a result of differences in the data.
elements.sent-2411-496: We try to isolate which dataset properties cause different kernel sizes in the first layer to be better or worse.
elements.sent-2411-497: This leads to two types of data manipulation, the first of which is resampling.
elements.sent-2411-498: One practical question about how to tune the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel CNN is whether to resize the convolutional filters, particularly in the first layer, when the sampling rate changes.
elements.sent-2411-499: Fortunately, the sampling rate is something that we can control artificially.
elements.sent-2411-500: We resample windows of vibration data to a lower rate, for example going from an initial window sampled at 48 kHz to a window of data which now contains the same content but is sampled at 24 kHz instead.
elements.sent-2411-501: The highest frequencies will be lost, but otherwise the signal will be preserved with high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-502: Simply it will take fewer data points to record the same back-and-forth vibrations, and each data point will represent a bigger jump forward in time from the previous one.
elements.sent-2411-503: To investigate the impact of the sampling rate on hyperparameters such as the kernel size of the first layer, we make multiple copies and resample them by a factor of 2, 4, 8 and 16.
elements.sent-2411-504: Starting off with data sampled at 48 kHz, this results in versions sampled at 24 kHz, 12 kHz, 6 kHz and 3 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-505: On each version of the data, we run a grid search over the hyperparameters which previous work showed were the most important.
elements.sent-2411-506: The grid search results can be analysed to compare what hyperparameter settings work best at different sampling rates.
elements.sent-2411-507: For each combination of possible hyperparameters in our grid search, we train and test on the original and the four resampled versions of the CWRU 48 kHz dataset.
elements.sent-2411-508: The CWRU data was chosen because it combines: a relatively high sampling rate, a well-documented and high-quality data collection procedure, remaining a moderate challenge for neural networks, and being perhaps the most widely used benchmark in the field.
elements.sent-2411-509: It has been suggested in past work that the reason a wide kernel boosts the accuracy of a neural network is that wide kernels are good at filtering out high-frequency noise.
elements.sent-2411-510: We investigate this by employing a lowpass filter to deliberately remove high frequencies from the data.
elements.sent-2411-511: If the main accomplishment of a wide kernel is to filter out high frequencies, then a larger range of kernel sizes should become viable if the high frequencies are already eliminated by pre-filtering.
elements.sent-2411-512: We do not know what (if any) cutoff should be used to decide which frequencies to keep and which to throw away, so we try multiple cutoff thresholds, starting from near the upper limit provided by the Nyquist frequency.
elements.sent-2411-513: In real terms, given data like CWRU sampled at 48 kHz, we apply filtering to multiple copies of the data.
elements.sent-2411-514: We filter with cutoff frequencies of 12 kHz, 6 kHz, 3 kHz, 1500 Hz, 750 Hz, 375 Hz, 187 Hz, 93 Hz and 46 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-515: An additional benefit of using many cutoff thresholds is that we can also check if any of them are too drastic, shedding light on which frequencies are essential for accurate fault detection and cannot be removed.
elements.sent-2411-516: It should be noted that filtering in this way does not impact the sampling rate or the size of a window of vibration data, it simply removes content which occurs at high frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-517: This makes it easy to compare the results when performing a grid search over the most important hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-518: A grid search can be performed on each filtered version of the CWRU dataset, and the results are directly comparable since the same architectures are applied to data which has exactly the same size and shape.
elements.subsubsection-2411-018: Analysis Techniques on Resampled and Filtered Data
elements.sent-2411-519: We analyse the resulting information in multiple ways.
elements.sent-2411-520: First, we look at correlations between different versions of the dataset, which gives an indication of the impact that resampling had.
elements.sent-2411-521: If resampling had no impact at all, then we would expect to have a perfect correlation of 1.0, and values lower than this would imply that resampling has an influence on which hyperparameter combinations are stronger and which are weaker.
elements.sent-2411-522: Afterwards, we look at how important different hyperparameters are.
elements.sent-2411-523: We do this by initially asking a related question of whether the test accuracy of a network can be predicted from the hyperparameters it has.
elements.sent-2411-524: If the accuracy can be predicted, we can hone in on the specifics of how much each hyperparameter contributes to the predictability.
elements.sent-2411-525: Correlation analysis: By resampling the CWRU data, we create multiple versions of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-526: One obvious question that arises is whether a combination of hyperparameters that works well on one version of the data will also work well on another.
elements.sent-2411-527: A similar question could be asked about the worst-performing hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-528: In general, this is a question of correlation: to what extent do the accuracy scores obtained on one dataset version correlate to the scores on data resampled to a different rate? We compute and then display the correlations, which gives an insight into how much the hyperparameters are affected by the resampling/filtering.
elements.sent-2411-529: One possibility is that certain hyperparameter combinations are simply better than others, and it does not matter if the properties of the data (such as sampling rate) change.
elements.sent-2411-530: In the extreme, this would result in a correlation of 1.0 between all versions of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-531: However, if smaller correlations are found, then it would suggest that the properties of the data impact which hyperparameters are best, or that there is some degree of randomness in the accuracy scores that deflates the correlations.
elements.sent-2411-532: Box plots: One of the key questions in our research is whether the optimal kernel size in the first layer changes when the data is either resampled or filtered.
elements.sent-2411-533: Box plots provide a straightforward view onto how well different kernel sizes tend to work.
elements.sent-2411-534: We plot the distribution of accuracy scores for each kernel size when applied to each version of the data.
elements.sent-2411-535: Feature importance based on Shapley values: After performing a grid search over different values that the hyperparameters can take, we are left with a set of accuracy scores.
elements.sent-2411-536: Each entry in the results records the specific values that were used for the three hyperparameters manipulated in the grid search, plus the trained network's accuracy on test data.
elements.sent-2411-537: We use these four pieces of information to investigate the relative importance of each hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-538: The analysis proceeds by analysing one grid search at a time, before comparing across grid searches.
elements.sent-2411-539: Remember that a grid search represents a certain level of resampling or filtering, e.g. it tells us what happens when different hyperparameter values are tried out on CWRU data resampled from 48 kHz to 24 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-540: Another grid search covers CWRU data resampled from 48 to 12 kHz, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-541: Here, we provide one particular approach to quantifying the importance of hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-542: Our approach asks how much each hyperparameter seems to contribute to the final accuracy of a network.
elements.sent-2411-543: If a hyperparameter is important, then setting it correctly tends to result in a noticeably higher accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-544: If a hyperparameter is not important, then even changing it drastically will not cause any increase or decrease in how accurate the network is.
elements.sent-2411-545: It is difficult to directly state how much a hyperparameter influences the accuracy of the network because this can be contingent based on other hyperparameters or could be subject to a nonlinear relationship which is hard to identify using basic statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-546: Our past work showed that nonlinear regression methods were noticeably better at the task of predicting how accurate networks will be.
elements.sent-2411-547: In order to model the obscure, nonlinear relationships between hyperparameters and accuracy, we use a multilayer perceptron.
elements.sent-2411-548: It is a simple neural network -- ours contains two hidden layers of 32 neurons -- which is told the how the hyperparameters of a wide-kernel CNN have been configured and is trained to predict what accuracy those hyperparameters lead to.
elements.sent-2411-549: The outputs from grid searches provide the data used for training the MLP.
elements.sent-2411-550: Once the MLP is trained, it contains information about the importance of different hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-551: In order to probe the MLP further we train versions which have access to a hyperparameter (i.e., are told what value the hyperparameter has) and versions which do not.
elements.sent-2411-552: If the versions without access to a hyperparameter perform much worse then the hyperparameter is seen as important.
elements.sent-2411-553: Shapley values provide a framework for fairly combining information from all the possible permutations of inclusions/exclusions of hyperparameters \cite{shapley1953value .}
elements.sent-2411-554: By visualising these Shapley values, we can look into the relative importance of each single hyperparameter, measured in terms of how much it contributes to reducing the MLP regressor's Mean Absolute Error (MAE).
elements.sent-2411-555: This is the method we employed earlier in \cite{hoogen2023hyperparams , although there we applied it to raw benchmark data rather than filtered and resampled CWRU data.}
elements.sent-2411-556: Calculating the Shapley value of a hyperparameter is done by permuting the order of all hyperparameters and then performing two regressions (using the MLP): one with the current hyperparameter included and one without.
elements.sent-2411-557: The difference in error between these two regressions indicates the hyperparameter's contribution to the overall model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-558: By averaging over all possible permutations, we obtain the precise Shapley value.
elements.sent-2411-559: For an approximation, a set number of permutations can be chosen at random.
elements.sent-2411-560: For the sake of computational efficiency, we use 100 random permutations.
elements.sent-2411-561: As seen in~\cite{brandsaeter2022shapley , Shapley values tend to converge quite quickly to a reasonable approximation, and so we do not feel it is necessary to run all permutations.}
elements.sent-2411-562: Following the notation in~\cite{brandsaeter2022shapley , the calculation can be written down follows: \begin{equation}\varphi_i = \frac{1}{\lvert N \rvert!} \sum_{\mathcal{O} \in \pi(\lvert N \rvert)} [v(Pre^i(\mathcal{O}) \cup \{i\}) - v(Pre^i(\mathcal{O}))]\end{equation} where $\varphi_i$ is the Shapley value of feature $i$, $\vert N \rvert$ is the number of features, $\mathcal{O}$ is a randomly-chosen permutation of the features, $v(s)$ is the average test performance (mean absolute error or MAE) of a regression using only the set of features $s$, and $Pre^i(\mathcal{O )$ is the set of features that appear earlier than feature $i$ in the permutation $\mathcal{O}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-563: Feature importance based on d-dimensional earth mover's distance: We want to understand how much the accuracy of the neural network changes depending on specific settings of the hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-564: To do this, we first look at how to summarise a single setting of a single hyperparameter, such as giving the filter in the first layer a width of 256.
elements.sent-2411-565: When the first layer has a filter of width 256, many possibilities remain for the other hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-566: Within our grid search, all the possibilities are exhaustively tested, the upshot of which is that we have a collection of many tested networks that all have a first layer filter width of 256.
elements.sent-2411-567: The test accuracy scores from a collection of networks, taken together, form a distribution.
elements.sent-2411-568: Some accuracy scores will be more frequent than others.
elements.sent-2411-569: The idea of our analysis is to take different distributions and compare them.
elements.sent-2411-570: We can compare the distribution of accuracy scores when the first layer has a filter size of 256 to when the filter size is 128, for example.
elements.sent-2411-571: If two distributions are wildly different, it clearly shows that changing the hyperparameter has caused a change in what accuracy you can expect from the network.
elements.sent-2411-572: To summarise this information, we use a metric called the d-dimensional Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) for each hyperparameter \cite{KLINE2019128 .}
elements.sent-2411-573: The EMD essentially measures the difference between two probability distributions, reflecting how much one needs to be adjusted to become the other.
elements.sent-2411-574: Higher EMD values indicate greater dissimilarity.
elements.sent-2411-575: The EMD is the same as the Wasserstein difference when comparing two distributions, but it can be extended to compare more than two distributions (hence `d-dimensional').
elements.sent-2411-576: Analysing all the values that a hyperparameter can take, we come up with a single EMD that summarises the hyperparameter overall.
elements.sent-2411-577: With EMD, we can assign a single number to each hyperparameter, representing its impact on accuracy scores.
elements.sent-2411-578: This is a method we used earlier in \cite{hudson2024stay , although there we applied it to raw benchmark data rather than filtered and resampled CWRU data.}
elements.sent-2411-579: It is important to note that some hyperparameters are tested with more values than others are.
elements.sent-2411-580: For instance, we might test five kernel sizes but only three strides and this makes the EMD values somewhat inconsistent and difficult to interpret.
elements.sent-2411-581: To make the results more usable we add an extra processing step that begins by calculating a baseline EMD for each hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-582: We do this by calculating the EMD for surrogate data in which the accuracy scores have been shuffled and therefore the relationships between hyperparameters and accuracy have been destroyed.
elements.sent-2411-583: Shuffling multiple times and recalculating the EMD allows us to estimate the average EMD due to chance.
elements.sent-2411-584: When we present the EMD for each hyperparameter we make the numbers more comparable and meaningful by dividing by the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-585: Across the various parts of our research, multiple datasets are employed.
elements.sent-2411-586: For the sake of clarity we now present the full collection of datasets we use and remark on which architectures they are used with.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tabular}{lr} Dataset Used With Unmanipulated benchmarks CWRU LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN Gearbox LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN MFPT LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN Paderborn LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN SEU LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN UOC LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN XJTU LSTM, Transformer, Wide-kernel CNN Resampling CWRU 48 kHz resampled to 24 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz resampled to 12 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz resampled to 6 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz resampled to 3 kHz Wide-kernel CNN Filtering CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 12 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 6 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 3 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 1500 Hz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 750 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 375 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 187 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 93 kHz Wide-kernel CNN CWRU 48 kHz lowpass at 46 kHz Wide-kernel CNN \end{tabular} \caption{Summary of datasets used in the research.} \label{tab:datasets_summary}
elements.sent-2411-588: Here we analyse the outputs from our grid search over hyperparameters, which has been applied to multiple datasets and to multiple filtered and resampled versions of the CWRU dataset.
elements.sent-2411-589: Combining these analyses leads to a broader understanding of how to set hyperparameters under different conditions.
elements.sent-2411-590: Before doing that however, we first attempt to establish the importance of hyperparameter tuning outside the context of a wide-kernel CNN by looking at how alternative architectures perform while their hyperparameters change.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Results Pt. 1 -- Experiments on Multiple Architectures
elements.sent-2411-591: The overall focus of our work is a specific wide-kernel CNN architecture.
elements.sent-2411-592: However, it is important to note that other architectures also present the user with hyperparameter decisions, and also suffer from the fact that poor hyperparameter choices can be disastrous for accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-593: We therefore briefly present some experiments with two other popular architectural styles: a recurrent LSTM type of neural network and a transformer network.
elements.sent-2411-594: These results establish the general importance of hyperparameter tuning when working with neural networks, which is the basis for the remaining sections where we deal with the wide-kernel architecture much more extensively.
elements.sent-2411-595: Across our sample of 100 LSTM configurations, we see that poor hyperparameters can be disastrous.
elements.sent-2411-596: In the worst cases (CWRU and XJTU) the lowest-performing network was wrong more than 10 times as often as the best network.
elements.sent-2411-597: On all datasets the default values do roughly as well as about 75\% of modified hyperparameter settings and therefore are reasonable choices.
elements.sent-2411-598: One interesting thing is that the LSTM network’s default values appear to under-perform on XJTU data.
elements.sent-2411-599: Facing this benchmark, an LSTM configured with the defaults only achieved an accuracy of 72\% whilst it was possible to get above 90\% with other hyperparameter values.
elements.sent-2411-600: Re-tuning the default hyperparameter settings seems to be a good idea on XJTU data, indicating that hyperparameters that perform well on one dataset do not necessarily perform well on another dataset.
elements.sent-2411-601: The default values fared relatively much better on CWRU and SEU.
elements.sent-2411-602: Overall the results show that hyperparameter tuning has a noticeable impact on the LSTM architecture much like with the transformer and wide-kernel CNN networks.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr} {} Min 25\% Median 75\% Max w/ Defaults CWRU 35 59 68 80 94 81 Gearbox 6 9 11 13 17 14 MFPT 46 49 50 50 54 52 Paderborn 28 40 46 50 57 48 SEU 62 88 96 98 99 94 UOC 24 35 41 50 72 54 XJTU 26 48 57 75 97 72 \end{tabular} \caption{Tukey's five-number summary of how accurate the LSTM architecture was with different hyperparameter settings. The accuracy score when using the default hyperparameter values is also shown in the right-hand column.} \label{tab:lstm_accuracies}
elements.sent-2411-603: The transformer architecture performs relatively well when using the default hyperparameter settings, as shown in Table \ref{tab:faultformer_accuracies .}
elements.sent-2411-604: On four of the baselines it was able to correctly identify fault conditions on more than 90\% of test cases.
elements.sent-2411-605: Looking across all of the baseline datasets, the default hyperparameter settings were at least as good as 75\% of altered versions of the settings.
elements.sent-2411-606: The results suggest that the default values are quite reasonable choices.
elements.sent-2411-607: On the Gearbox dataset the transformer was inaccurate no matter if the default hyperparameter settings were used or not, suggesting a specific problem with that one dataset.
elements.sent-2411-608: Importantly, our results show overall that the transformer architecture is sensitive to hyperparameter tweaking.
elements.sent-2411-609: On CWRU and UOC for example, the accuracy plunges by roughly 90\% when swapping the best-performing hyperparameters for the worst.
elements.sent-2411-610: Overall, the transformer architecture beats the LSTM architecture so long as it is given the right hyperparameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-611: The transformer was more sensitive to changes in the hyperparameters our study explored, in the sense that it could be very accurate or be very inaccurate depending on whether those were right.
elements.sent-2411-612: However, getting the hyperparameters right paid off for the transformer since its max accuracy was 40\% higher than the LSTM’s max when testing on UOC data.
elements.sent-2411-613: Given the correct hyperparameter settings, the transformer could be at least as good as any LSTM network we tried out on the seven benchmarks.

The accuracy score when using the default hyperparameter values is also shown in the right-hand column.} \label{tab:faultformer_accuracies}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Results Pt. 2 -- Focusing on the Wide-Kernel CNN Architecture
elements.sent-2411-614: The main body of our research is about the wide-kernel CNN architecture described in Section \ref{sec:method:widekernelarchitecture .}
elements.sent-2411-615: We begin the results by revisiting how successful networks in a grid search are when trained and tested on seven different benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-616: This grid search is the same as was analysed before in \cite{hudson2024stay , although here our results have a different focus.}
elements.sent-2411-617: We look at the average test results when setting individual hyperparameters to specific values, and we also zoom in on the interactions between the hyperparameters that were previously identified as most important.
elements.sent-2411-618: These results help us formulate guidance on how to tune the hyperparameters of the wide-kernel CNN, in later sections of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-619: After revisiting the grid search applied to seven benchmark datasets we shift our attention to how well wide-kernel CNNs work on manipulated versions of the CWRU data.
elements.sent-2411-620: Deliberately manipulating the data is a means to get greater control of data properties and see how they influence which hyperparameter values are best.
elements.sent-2411-621: We maintain a special interest in the kernel size in the first layer (the `wide kernel' of the wide-kernel architecture) because this seems to be the most data-dependent hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-622: The other hyperparameters of the network appear fairly consistent in terms of what is the correct way to tune them, but the first layer kernel size sometimes needs to be shrunk and sometimes needs to be expanded.
elements.sent-2411-623: By first resampling and then filtering we look at whether the sampling rate and the spectral content have an impact on what kernel size is best.
elements.sent-2411-624: Now we look at how the accuracy of the wide-kernel CNN varies as we change both the hyperparameter values and the data.
elements.sent-2411-625: Each condition's accuracy is shown in Table \ref{tab:hyperparameters_datasets_averages .}
elements.sent-2411-626: These results are based on the same grid search used previously in \cite{hudson2024stay , which trained and tested different combinations of hyperparameters on 7 benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-627: We revisit the grid search in order to pull out and highlight helpful information when thinking about how to tune the hyperparameters on different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-628: First, we see that the accuracy tends to be higher on some benchmark datasets than others.
elements.sent-2411-629: It seems that some tasks were more difficult or were more demanding within the constraints imposed by the grid search.
elements.sent-2411-630: Differences are not unexpected however, since each dataset is distinct in terms of recording procedure and number and type of faults.
elements.sent-2411-631: Perhaps more interesting are the individual accuracy scores showing what happens when we tune different hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-632: The kernel size in the first layer reveals the most unexpected results.
elements.sent-2411-633: With CWRU, Paderborn and UOC there is a strong effect whereby a longer kernel does better and the longest kernel exceeds the shortest by at least 15\% accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-634: The MFPT and XJTU benchmarks exhibit a weaker effect of roughly 6\% difference between the longest and shortest kernel.
elements.sent-2411-635: The kernel size in layer 1 barely effects the results on Gearbox data.
elements.sent-2411-636: Meanwhile, a strong contradictory effect is observed when using SEU data.
elements.sent-2411-637: The shortest kernel is much better (more than 30\% better) compared to the longest kernel size.
elements.sent-2411-638: In all, it seems that longer kernels in the first layer tend to be best but it very much depends on the fault vibration data.
elements.sent-2411-639: The stride in the first layer is a simpler story.
elements.sent-2411-640: In the case of Gearbox it does not affect the accuracy by more than 5\% whilst in all the other datasets it is clear that the highest value (16) is best.
elements.sent-2411-641: A greater stride reduces computation by shrinking the outputs and potentially removes redundancy by decreasing the overlap of the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-642: The number of filters in the first layer was also identified as important by past work.
elements.sent-2411-643: Good performance is often achieved when there are 16 or 32 filters in the first layer.
elements.sent-2411-644: Either 16 or 32 was the best on every dataset except XJTU (when 8 filters was 1\% better) and Gearbox.
elements.sent-2411-645: With Gearbox, the trend is that more filters gives higher accuracy and 256 beats 8 filters by about 7\% accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-646: Looking across all the datasets, 16 or 32 are the safest bets when tuning this hyperparameter but exceptions are possible.
elements.sent-2411-647: The final `important' hyperparameter is the number of filters in layer 3 to 5.
elements.sent-2411-648: We observe that either 16 or 32 works best on all the benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-649: This conclusion at least is straightforward.
elements.subsubsection-2411-022: Influence of Important Hyperparameters on One Another
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{bin/influences_graph_3_var.pdf} \caption{The likelihood that tuning one hyperparameter (source of an arrow) will cause another (destination of an arrow) to need re-tuning. } \label{fig:influence_3_var}
elements.sent-2411-650: We now take another deep dive into the three hyperparameters identified as important by our previous work.
elements.sent-2411-651: It is very computationally expensive to run a full grid search over all the possible hyperparameter combinations and so someone applying the network to new data might well want to know how to tune the hyperparameters one-at-a-time.
elements.sent-2411-652: We ask which is the best order to tune the three most important hyperparameters sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-653: The method we used in our previous paper \cite{hudson2024stay comes in handy again here: we look at how much one hyperparameter influences another hyperparameter which has already been tuned.}
elements.sent-2411-654: Because the hyperparameters are interconnected it is possible that a hyperparameter that started off being optimal no longer has the best value when a different hyperparameter changes.
elements.sent-2411-655: We look at how often this phenomenon happens and we summarise the information as an `influence' score.
elements.sent-2411-656: If A is highly influential on B then changing A most likely means changing the best value for B.
elements.sent-2411-657: We can see the numbers in Figure \ref{fig:influence_3_var .}
elements.sent-2411-658: The kernel size in layer 1 appears to be the most influential and therefore it appears to be the best one to tune first.
elements.sent-2411-659: Tuning it later on would likely disrupt the other hyperparameters and cause them to need re-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-660: In other words, there is often no point in tuning the number of filters in layers 1 or 3-5 beforehand, since they will need to be tuned again when the kernel size changes.
elements.sent-2411-661: After choosing the size of filters in the first layer it is best to choose how many next.
elements.sent-2411-662: The choice of how many filters in layers 3 to 5 can be delayed to the end.
elements.sent-2411-663: The results of a grid search over the three most important hyperparameters, performed once each for the different resampling conditions, are brought together in the results here.
elements.sent-2411-664: Visualising the results makes it possible to spot any obvious trends in how the (increasing levels of) resampling impact the performance of different hyperparameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-665: We present several analyses on both CWRU 48 kHz data to see how much each hyperparameter seems to influence performance.
elements.sent-2411-666: These analyses include using a method based on Shapley values and using a method based on d-dimensional earth mover's scores to pinpoint important hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-667: Here, we include the results obtained when resampling CWRU data, starting from data originally sampled at 48 kHz.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{bin/resampled_48kHz/correlation_matrix.pdf} \caption{Correlation between different resampling conditions, starting from 48 kHz. } \label{fig:correlations_resampling_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-668: First, we consider how much the accuracy scores change as a consequence of resampling the data.
elements.sent-2411-669: Correlations tell us whether the best-performing combinations of hyperparameters on one version of the data also remain the best after resampling, and vice versa with the worst performers.
elements.sent-2411-670: The precise correlation results are shown in Figure \ref{fig:correlations_resampling_48kHz .}
elements.sent-2411-671: All correlations are positive which implies that the hyperparameter values that do well on one version of the data have a tendency to perform well on data resampled to a different rate.
elements.sent-2411-672: The weakest correlation is between the original 48 kHz data and the most resampled data of 3 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-673: This is not surprising since they are the furthest away from each other in terms of data properties.
elements.sent-2411-674: The weakened correlation between 48 kHz and 3 kHz gives us an initial Before moving on we must make a brief disclaimer about the correlations, in particular we acknowledge there is a ceiling effect because many networks achieve close to the maximum accuracy of 1.0, and, the training and testing of neural networks is stochastic and so there may be some random noise in the accuracy scores.
elements.sent-2411-675: There is a chance that these statistical quirks have inflated or deflated the correlations, and so we do not report p-values which might be biased by these effects.
elements.sent-2411-676: Instead, we view the results as indicative.
elements.sent-2411-677: The results give an indication that hyperparameter settings that are stronger on one version of the data are more likely to be stronger when also used on a resampled version of the data, but room is also left for differences between sampling rates to emerge.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.63\textwidth]{bin/resampled_48kHz/kernelsize_boxplots.pdf} \caption{Accuracy scores for different kernel sizes in the first convolutional layer, as tested on different resampling conditions for data originally sampled at 48 kHz. } \label{fig:kernelsize_resampling_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-678: We take a closer look at the kernel size hyperparameter in particular since we want to know if the kernel size needs to change when the sampling rate is altered.
elements.sent-2411-679: The box plots in Figure \ref{fig:kernelsize_resampling_48kHz let us see more clearly which specific kernel sizes are best and not only how important the hyperparameter is overall.}
elements.sent-2411-680: These plots confirm that larger kernels perform better, with 128 or 256 being the best even when resampling from 48 kHz to 3 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-681: Resampling condenses the data and makes the inputs shorter and one might expect smaller kernels to become more effective as a result of increased resampling.
elements.sent-2411-682: The results however show no such trend.
elements.sent-2411-683: When looking at the box plots we also observe that the task becomes more difficult as the data is resampled further.
elements.sent-2411-684: Either the loss of information that comes with compression or the impact of reducing the length of inputs could explain the downwards trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-685: Considering the results in Figure \ref{fig:kernelsize_resampling_48kHz , resampling to 24 kHz at least seems to have no negative impact and so it could be unnecessary and wasteful to record at 48 kHz when a lower sampling frequency is equally good.}
elements.sent-2411-686: Further research would be needed to establish this more firmly.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{bin/resampled_48kHz/shapley_heatmap.pdf} \caption{Feature importance after resampling to different rates, starting from 48 kHz. Each row is normalised to show the relative importance of the hyperparameters. } \label{fig:shapley_resampling_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-687: In the next piece of our analysis we look at feature importance.
elements.sent-2411-688: This shows us the impact of resampling on the other two most important hyperparameters alongside kernel size in the first layer.
elements.sent-2411-689: We interpret importance by saying that if a hyperparameter is important then it determines whether a neural network is accurate or not.
elements.sent-2411-690: If a hyperparameter is important and you know its value, then you can predict the accuracy of the network.
elements.sent-2411-691: We arrive at numeric importance scores by looking at the contribution (Shapley value) of each hyperparameter to the successful predictions of accuracy of a multilayer perceptron.
elements.sent-2411-692: Figure \ref{fig:shapley_resampling_48kHz shows the Shapley values after normalising each row to add up to 1.0.}
elements.sent-2411-693: Naturally, higher values indicate more importance.
elements.sent-2411-694: No matter the degree of resampling, the number of filters in layers 3-5 is the most important.
elements.sent-2411-695: Interestingly, this hyperparameter is increasingly dominant as the resampling gets more extreme.
elements.sent-2411-696: With no resampling or with resampling to 24 kHz, the other hyperparameters constitute almost half of the Shapley values.
elements.sent-2411-697: There is no obvious pattern to the kernel size, and the much higher importance when resampling to 24 kHz is an unexplained outlier.
elements.sent-2411-698: In any case, the kernel size did not keep on becoming more important as a result of escalating resampling.
elements.sent-2411-699: Instead, the clearest conclusion is that the filters in the later layers become more important when resampling is greater.
elements.sent-2411-700: If we consider that the first layer is often used to process raw, noisy signals then the declining importance of this layer might reflect the fact that more resampled data is already more processed before it reaches the neural network as an input.
elements.sent-2411-701: Alternatively, networks' overall lower accuracies on highly resampled data (shown in Figure \ref{fig:kernelsize_resampling_48kHz ) present the possibility that resampling deletes patterns in the signal, hence the first layer is important when processing mildly resampled data since it allows networks to pick up on patterns useful for successfully classifying vibrations.}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{bin/resampled_48kHz/wasserstein_heatmap.pdf} \caption{How much the accuracy scores change as a result of tweaking a hyperparameter, when processing data with different levels of resampling, starting from 48 kHz. Each cell is normalised to show its value relative to surrogate data (see explanation in Section \ref{sec:EMD_method}). } \label{fig:wasserstein_resampling_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-702: Another way to consider how important different hyperparameters are is to ask how much the accuracy scores change when the hyperparameter is changed.
elements.sent-2411-703: The d-dimensional earth mover's distance (EMD) quantifies just this.
elements.sent-2411-704: Figure \ref{fig:wasserstein_resampling_48kHz shows how many times larger the observed EMD is than a surrogate baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-705: Firstly we see that compared to baseline values, the number of filters in the first layer has little impact.
elements.sent-2411-706: The number of filters in layers 3-5 however are much more important no matter what resampling has been applied.
elements.sent-2411-707: The size of the filter in the first layer is interesting because it seems important with no resampling or little resampling, but becomes unimportant when resampling to 12 kHz or less.
elements.sent-2411-708: In this section, instead of resampling we look at filtering the data.
elements.sent-2411-709: Filtering modifies the information that is included in the data such that certain frequencies are removed, whilst holding constant the sampling rate and the time series length.
elements.sent-2411-710: This gives us some companion results to the resampling results we just showed.
elements.sent-2411-711: The filtered data isolates the impact of the content of the signals whereas the previous resampling also made the sequences shorter and more condensed.
elements.sent-2411-712: When processing data sampled at 48 kHz we have nine filtering conditions: low-pass with a threshold of 12 kHz, 6 kHz, 3 kHz, 1.5 kHz, 750 Hz, 375 Hz, 187 Hz, 93 Hz and 46 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-713: These can be compared side by side.
elements.sent-2411-714: We use the methods described earlier, namely employing Shapley values and the d-dimensional earth mover's scores, to investigate the impact of filtering.
elements.sent-2411-715: It had been suggested that a wide kernel is beneficial primarily because it filters out high-frequency noise.
elements.sent-2411-716: The filtering experiments make it possible to verify whether this really is the benefit granted by a wide kernel.
elements.sent-2411-717: Here, we include the results obtained when filtering CWRU data, applying different thresholds to decide how many frequencies to filter out.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{bin/filtered_48kHz/correlation_matrix.pdf} \caption{Correlation between filtering conditions with 48 kHz CWRU data. } \label{fig:correlations_filtering_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-718: Correlations between the different conditions appear in Figure \ref{fig:correlations_filtering_48kHz .}
elements.sent-2411-719: We see that many levels of filtering, especially the weakest forms that only remove the highest frequencies, give correlations in the range 0.5 to 0.8.
elements.sent-2411-720: These quite high correlations imply that moderate filtering did not have a large impact on how to set the hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-721: Filtering at a threshold lower than 187 Hz however causes a sudden drop in correlations.
elements.sent-2411-722: The most extreme forms of filtering evidently do affect which hyperparameter settings work well.
elements.sent-2411-723: We observe that the average accuracy is much lower when filtering at 46 or 93 Hz (the average accuracy was 0.29 in both cases) than when filtering at 3000 Hz or above (the averages ranged from 0.95 to 0.97), and there might simply be no distinction between good and bad hyperparameter settings when using extreme filtering.
elements.sent-2411-724: If there are no good and bad hyperparameter settings then the results will be more influenced by noise and will correlate less, which is one possible explanation for the relatively low correlations seen.
elements.sent-2411-725: In summary, hyperparameters generalise fairly well and so the same settings do well on multiple levels of filtering, except when the strongest filtering is applied.
elements.sent-2411-726: Filtering changed the data properties but did not completely redefine which settings are good or bad.

elements.sent-2411-727: In order to make a deeper dive into the kernel size in the first layer we can plot the accuracy scores visually for each kernel size.
elements.sent-2411-728: Figure \ref{fig:kernelsize_filtering_48kHz shows the spread of accuracy scores obtained when using different lengths of kernel and when processing data with different levels of filtering.}
elements.sent-2411-729: If the filtering is anything milder than 93 Hz we see that a larger kernel performs better.
elements.sent-2411-730: The value of a wide kernel does not relate to high-frequency noise as was suspected because any such noise would be removed by the filters.
elements.sent-2411-731: It seems that the benefit of a wide kernel must be explained in some other way.

Each row is normalised to show the relative importance of the hyperparameters. } \label{fig:shapley_filtering_48kHz}
elements.sent-2411-732: We derive Shapley values that represent how much each hyperparameter helps when predicting how accurate a network will be.
elements.sent-2411-733: These importance values are large when a hyperparameter really tells us something about how well a network will perform.
elements.sent-2411-734: Figure \ref{fig:shapley_filtering_48kHz shows Shapley values derived from networks trained to classify faults from vibration data with different levels of filtering.}
elements.sent-2411-735: Both the size of the first filters and the number of filters in the later layers are important.
elements.sent-2411-736: Note that the balance of importance between these two hyperparameters changes as filtering is increasingly applied but it changes without any obvious pattern.
